title: Supervised vs Unsupervised Anomaly Detection
concept: When you have labeled examples of past anomalies, you can train a supervised model on them; when you don't, you fall back to unsupervised methods that define "normal" from the data alone.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does distance-based anomaly detection decide a point is anomalous?

options.text: By checking whether its nearest neighbors are far away
By checking whether it has a label attached to it
By checking whether it was collected on a weekend

exercises.explain: Right! Distance-based detection flags points whose nearest neighbors are unusually far away.

cards.title: The detective with a mugshot book vs. the rookie on patrol
cards.body: One detective has a book of known criminals' faces to check against — spot a match, make the call. A rookie on a quiet new beat has no such book; they have to learn what "normal" looks like on their own before anything unusual stands out.

cards.title: Two very different starting points
cards.body: Supervised anomaly detection uses labeled examples of past anomalies (like confirmed fraud cases) to train a model that recognizes similar ones. Unsupervised anomaly detection has no such labels — it must define "normal" purely from the pattern in the data, then flag whatever breaks it.

cards.title: A doctor who's seen the disease vs. one who hasn't
cards.body: A doctor who has treated 500 confirmed cases of a disease learns its exact signs — that's supervised, learning directly from labeled examples. A doctor facing a totally new illness with zero confirmed cases has to notice what's simply different from a healthy patient — that's unsupervised, working from "normal" alone.

cards.title: Fraud with history vs. a factory with none
cards.body: A bank with years of confirmed fraud cases can train a supervised model directly on those labels. A brand-new factory machine with zero recorded failures has no such history — engineers must use unsupervised methods, defining "normal" vibration and temperature from healthy operation alone.

cards.title: It's not that one approach is always better
cards.body: It's tempting to think supervised is simply the "upgraded" version of unsupervised. In reality, the choice depends entirely on whether labeled anomalies exist. Rare events like catastrophic failures often never build up enough labeled examples, so unsupervised methods remain essential even for mature systems.

left.text:
Labeled past anomalies exist
Trains on known examples
e.g. confirmed fraud cases

right.text:
No anomaly labels exist
Defines "normal" from data
e.g. a brand-new sensor

exercises.prompt: A bank has thousands of transactions confirmed as fraud from past investigations and wants to train a model directly on those labeled cases. Which approach is this?
exercises.explain: Exactly! Training directly on confirmed, labeled past examples is supervised anomaly detection.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Unsupervised anomaly detection requires labeled examples of past anomalies to get started.
exercises.explain: False! Unsupervised anomaly detection defines "normal" purely from the data's own pattern, with no labels required.

exercises.sentence: Supervised anomaly detection trains on labeled examples, while unsupervised detection must define ___ from the data alone.
exercises.explain: Without labeled anomalies, an unsupervised method has to figure out what "normal" looks like directly from the data itself.

exercises.prompt: A newly launched app has zero historical examples of account takeovers, but security engineers still want to flag suspicious login patterns. Which approach fits, and why?

options.text: Unsupervised — there are no labeled examples yet, so "normal" login behavior has to be learned from the data itself
Supervised — you should wait and collect confirmed fraud labels for a year before doing anything
Neither approach works on tabular data

exercises.explain: Correct! With no labeled anomalies available, unsupervised methods let you start flagging unusual behavior immediately by learning normal patterns from the data.

exercises.prompt: A company has 10 years of confirmed insurance fraud cases for auto claims, but is now expanding into a brand-new insurance product with zero claims history. Which anomaly detection approach fits each situation?

options.text: Supervised for the mature auto claims (labeled fraud history exists) and unsupervised for the new product (no labels yet, must define normal from scratch)
Supervised for both, since supervised methods always work regardless of label availability
Unsupervised for both, since labels are never useful for anomaly detection
It doesn't matter which approach is used as long as the data is anomaly detection related

exercises.explain: Exactly! Supervised methods need labeled anomalies to train on, which the mature auto claims have. The new product has no history yet, so it needs an unsupervised approach that defines "normal" purely from its own data.
